questions.text: Which of the following cannot be oxidized by H₂O₂?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a mixture of potassium iodide (KI) and hydrochloric acid (HCl). KI can be oxidized by H2O2 to produce iodine, so it can be oxidized.
Ozone (O₃) cannot be oxidized by hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) under normal conditions. Ozone is already an oxidizing agent itself, and it is a relatively stable molecule.
PbS (lead(II) sulfide) can be oxidized by H2O2. H2O2 can react with PbS to produce lead sulfate (PbSO4) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) through an oxidation-reduction reaction.
Na2SO3 (sodium sulfite) can also be oxidized by H2O2. H2O2 can oxidize sulfite ions (SO32-) to sulfate ions (SO42-) in the presence of an acidic medium.

questions.explanation: Ozone (O₃) is a much stronger oxidizing agent than hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂). Therefore, H₂O₂ cannot oxidize O₃; instead, H₂O₂ would act as a reducing agent in a reaction with ozone.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds turns white on treatment with H2O2 ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: HgS (mercury sulfide, cinnabar) is typically black or red and is generally unreactive toward H₂O₂ under normal conditions. It does not turn white.
Lead(II) sulfide (PbS) turns white when treated with H2O2 as it forms lead sulfate (PbSO4), which is a white solid.
NiS (nickel sulfide) is a black compound and is also generally unreactive toward H₂O₂ in a way that would produce a white precipitate under the described conditions.
Copper(II) sulfide (CuS) does not turn white when treated with H₂O₂. It forms copper sulfate (CuSO₄), which is blue.

questions.explanation: Lead(II) sulfide (PbS) is a black solid. Hydrogen peroxide oxidizes it to lead(II) sulfate (PbSO₄), which is a white solid, via the reaction PbS + 4H₂O₂ → PbSO₄ + 4H₂O.

questions.text: An example of a covalent hydride is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: NaH (Sodium hydride) is an ionic hydride (or saline hydride). It is formed from a highly electropositive s-block metal (sodium) and hydrogen, where the metal transfers an electron to hydrogen, forming the H⁻ (hydride) anion.
CaH₂ (calcium hydride) is also an ionic hydride. It is formed from an s-block metal (calcium) and hydrogen. It contains calcium cations (Ca²⁺) and hydride anions (H⁻).
SrH₂ (Strontium hydride) is likewise an ionic hydride, as strontium (Sr) is an alkaline earth metal (s-block element) and forms ionic bonds with hydrogen, similar to CaH₂.

Arsine (AsH₃) is a covalent hydride. It is a compound in which arsenic (As) is bonded to three hydrogen (H) atoms through covalent bonds. Covalent hydrides are compounds in which hydrogen is covalently bonded to nonmetals or metalloids, and in the case of arsine, it is bonded to arsenic. Arsine is a colourless, highly toxic, and flammable gas with a garlic-like odour. It is used in certain industrial applications but must be handled with extreme care due to its toxicity.

questions.explanation: Covalent hydrides are formed when hydrogen bonds with nonmetals or metalloids. Arsine (AsH₃) is a covalent hydride because arsenic (As) is a metalloid. The other options are ionic (saline) hydrides formed with alkali or alkaline earth metals.

questions.text: Which of the following hydrides is electron-precise hydride?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: B2H6 is not an electron-precise hydride. Electron-precise hydrides involve main group elements where the central atom has exactly enough electrons to satisfy the octet rule, which is not the case here.
NH₃ is not an electron-precise hydride, as it has one lone pair of electrons, which does not fulfil the criteria of an electron-precise hydride.
Water (H2O) is not an electron-precise hydride due to its two lone pairs of electrons, which do not result in an electron-precise configuration for the central atom.

CH4 (Methane) is the correct answer. Methane is an electron-precise hydride of carbon where the central atom forms four covalent bonds with hydrogen, fulfilling the octet rule and achieving electron precision.

questions.explanation: Electron-precise hydrides have just enough electrons to form normal single bonds—no shortage, no extra lone pairs. CH₄ has exactly the electrons needed to form four regular C–H bonds. B₂H₆ is electron-deficient, while NH₃ and H₂O are electron-rich (they have lone pairs), so only CH₄ is electron-precise.

questions.text: The volume strength of Perhydrol is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The volume strength of Perhydrol is not 20, it is actually 100.
The volume strength of Perhydrol is not 30, it is actually 100.

Perhydrol, also known as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), is typically available in various concentrations, and its 'volume strength' is a measure of its concentration. A volume strength of 100 means that the hydrogen peroxide solution is 100 times more concentrated than a 1% solution. A 100-volume strength hydrogen peroxide solution is extremely concentrated and is typically used for industrial and chemical purposes. It is highly reactive and can be hazardous if not handled with care. When dealing with high-concentration hydrogen peroxide solutions, it is essential to follow safety precautions and handling procedures to avoid accidents.
The volume strength of Perhydrol is not 10, it is actually 100.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: 100. Perhydrol, also known as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), has a volume strength of 100, indicating a highly concentrated solution. This concentration is 100 times more than a 1% solution, making it suitable for industrial and chemical uses. Handling such high-concentration hydrogen peroxide solutions requires strict safety measures to prevent accidents.Options A, B, and D are incorrect as they provide different volume strength values for Perhydrol, which do not align with the actual concentration of 100.

questions.text: H2O2 cannot be synthesized by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Addition of ice cold H2SO4 on BaO2
options.explanation: H₂O₂ can be prepared by the addition of ice-cold H₂SO₄ to BaO₂.

options.text: Addition of ice cold H2SO4 on PbO2
options.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) cannot be prepared by adding ice-cold sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) to lead dioxide (PbO₂). This is because the reaction of PbO₂ with H₂SO₄ does not produce hydrogen peroxide. Instead, the reaction typically results in the formation of lead sulfate (PbSO4) and water (H2O):PbO2 + H2SO4 → PbSO4 + H2OIn this reaction, the lead dioxide is reacting with sulfuric acid to produce lead sulfate and water, not hydrogen peroxide.
H₂O₂ can be prepared by aerial oxidation of 2-ethylanthraquinol.

options.text: Electrolysis of (NH4)2SO4 at a high current density
options.explanation: It can be prepared by electrolysis of (NH₄)₂SO₄ at a high current density.

questions.explanation: Lead dioxide (PbO₂) is not a peroxide but a true oxide of lead in the +4 oxidation state. It reacts with sulfuric acid in an acid-base reaction, not a redox reaction that would produce hydrogen peroxide. The other methods listed are valid synthesis routes for H₂O₂.

questions.text: Atomic hydrogen reacts with oxygen to give:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: For water, hydrogen should be in molecular form, not atomic form. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Atomic hydrogen (H) can react with oxygen (O₂) to form hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂). The given reaction pathway explains the formation of hydrogen peroxide, making this the correct option.

options.text: A mixture of water and hydrogen peroxide
options.explanation: Only hydrogen peroxide is formed when atomic hydrogen reacts with oxygen, so this option is incorrect.
Atomic hydrogen reacting with oxygen does indeed form hydrogen peroxide, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Atomic hydrogen reacts with O₂ stepwise (H· → HO₂· → H₂O₂), and this pathway strongly favors hydrogen peroxide formation. Water forms only in minor amounts, so the product is almost pure H₂O₂.

questions.text: Which one of the following processes will produce hard water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Permanent hardness of water is due to chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium, i.e., CaCl₂, CaSO₄, MgCl₂, and MgSO₄. Saturation of water with MgCO3 does not produce hard water, as it does not introduce calcium or magnesium ions.

Permanent hardness of water is due to chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium, i.e. CaCl2, CaSO4, MgCl2 and MgSO4. Saturation of water with CaSO₄ can lead to the formation of calcium ions and sulfate ions (SO₄²⁻). This contributes to total hardness and produces hard water.
Addition of Na₂SO₄ (sodium sulfate) to water does not produce hard water, as it does not introduce calcium or magnesium ions, which are key contributors to water hardness.

CaCl₂, CaSO₄, MgCl₂, and MgSO₄ are the chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium that cause permanent hardness in water; saturation of water with CaCO3 does not result in hard water because it does not introduce calcium or magnesium ions.

questions.explanation: Hardness in water is caused by the presence of dissolved calcium (Ca²⁺) and magnesium (Mg²⁺) ions. Calcium sulfate (CaSO₄) is a salt that dissolves in water to produce Ca²⁺ ions, thus contributing to permanent hardness.

questions.text: In context with the industrial preparation of hydrogen from water gas (CO + H2), which of the following is the correct statement?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: CO and H2 are fractionally separated using Copper Chloride solution
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Copper Chloride solution is not used for the fractional separation of CO and H2 in the industrial preparation of hydrogen.

options.text: CO is removed by absorption in aqueous Cu2Cl2 solution
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Aqueous Cu₂Cl₂ solution is not used to absorb CO in this process.

options.text: H2 is removed through occlusion with Pd
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Palladium (Pd) occlusion is not used for removing H2 in the given industrial process.

options.text: CO is oxidized to CO2 with steam in the presence of a catalyst followed by absorption of CO2 in an alkaline solution
options.explanation: This option is correct. In the water-gas shift reaction, CO is converted to CO2 using steam and a catalyst, and CO2 is then removed using an alkaline solution.

questions.explanation: This describes the water-gas shift reaction. The initial mixture is treated with steam over a catalyst to convert the toxic CO into CO₂, producing more H₂. The CO₂ is then easily removed by scrubbing the gas mixture with an alkaline solution, leaving purified hydrogen.

questions.text: Why does H+ ion always get associated with other atoms or molecules?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The ionization enthalpy of hydrogen resembles that of the alkali metals
options.explanation: This option does not address why the H+ ion associates with other atoms or molecules. It focuses on ionization enthalpy, which is not directly related to the reactivity of the H+ ion.

options.text: Its reactivity is similar to halogens
options.explanation: While this option mentions the reactivity of hydrogen, it does not explain why the H+ ion specifically associates with other species. It does not address the core reason behind this behavior.

options.text: It resembles both alkali metals and halogens
options.explanation: This option highlights hydrogen's unique position in the periodic table but does not explain why the H+ ion associates with other atoms or molecules. It does not directly address the question's focus on the reactivity of the H+ ion.

options.text: Loss of an electron from a hydrogen atom results in a nucleus of a very small size
options.explanation: The H+ ion, being a lone proton, has a high charge density due to its small size and positive charge. This makes it highly reactive and eager to associate with other atoms or molecules to stabilize itself by forming bonds. This option provides a direct explanation for why the H+ ion always gets associated with other species.

questions.explanation: A hydrogen ion (H⁺) is a bare proton with no electrons. This gives it an incredibly small size and a very high positive charge density. As a result, it is extremely reactive and will immediately associate with any available electron pair, such as the lone pairs on a water molecule.

questions.text: Among LiH, NaH, KH, RbH, and CsH, the correct order of increasing ionic character is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The correct order of increasing ionic character among the metal hydrides is LiH < NaH < KH < RbH < CsH.

The ionic character of metal hydrides increases down a group in the periodic table due to larger metal atoms and a greater electronegativity difference. Therefore, the correct order is LiH < NaH < KH < RbH < CsH.
LiH < NaH < KH < RbH < CsH is the proper sequence of increasing ionic character among the metal hydrides.
The metal hydrides should be arranged in the following order of increasing ionic character: LiH < NaH < KH < RbH < CsH.

questions.explanation: The ionic character of a bond is determined by the difference in electronegativity between the two atoms. As you move down the alkali metal group, the electronegativity of the metal decreases, which increases the electronegativity difference between the metal and hydrogen. This leads to an increase in ionic character down the group.
hints: LiH, NaH, KH, RbH, and CsH

questions.text: Which of the following hydrides is electron-rich hydride?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: B₂H₆ is not an electron-rich hydride, as it does not contain lone pairs of electrons. Hence, this option is incorrect.
NH₃ is an electron-rich hydride, as it contains a lone pair of electrons. It acts as a Lewis base in chemical reactions. This option is partially correct.
H₂O is an electron-rich hydride, as it contains two lone pairs of electrons. It acts as a Lewis base and participates in hydrogen bonding. This option is partially correct.

This option is correct because both NH3 and H2O are electron-rich hydrides containing lone pairs of electrons. NH3 has a lone pair on nitrogen, while H2O has two lone pairs on oxygen, making them good Lewis bases and capable of various chemical reactions.

questions.explanation: Electron-rich hydrides are those where the central atom has one or more lone pairs of electrons after forming bonds with hydrogen. Both ammonia (NH₃, one lone pair) and water (H₂O, two lone pairs) fit this description. B₂H₆ is electron-deficient.

questions.text: Consider the reactions: (A) H₂O₂ + 2HI → I₂ + 2H₂O (B) HOCl + H₂O₂ → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻ + O₂. Which statement is correct about H₂O₂?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: An oxidizing agent in both (A) and (B)
options.explanation: In reaction (A), H₂O₂ acts as an oxidizing agent, but in reaction (B), it acts as a reducing agent. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: An oxidizing agent in (A) and a reducing agent in (B)
options.explanation: In reaction (A), the oxidation state of H2O2 changes from -1 to -2, indicating it is oxidizing iodine. In reaction (B), it changes from -1 to 0, indicating it is being oxidized itself, and thus acts as a reducing agent. This is the correct option.

options.text: A reducing agent in (A) and an oxidizing agent in (B)
options.explanation: In reaction (A), H2O2 acts as an oxidizing agent, not a reducing agent. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: A reducing agent in both (A) and (B)
options.explanation: H2O₂ acts as an oxidizing agent rather than a reducing agent in reaction (A). As a result, this choice is wrong.

questions.explanation: In reaction (A), H₂O₂ is reduced while oxidizing I⁻ to I₂, so it acts as an oxidizing agent. In reaction (B), H₂O₂ is oxidized to O₂ while reducing HOCl to Cl⁻, so it acts as a reducing agent. Thus, H₂O₂ is an oxidizing agent in (A) and a reducing agent in (B).

questions.text: Choose the mixture that can act as a rockets propellant.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The mixture of liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen is widely used as a rocket propellant due to its high efficiency and energetic output. It is known for producing a large amount of thrust and water as a clean byproduct, making it a preferred choice in the aerospace industry.

Liquid nitrogen is not used as a fuel in rocket propulsion. While liquid oxygen serves as an oxidizer, the combination with liquid nitrogen does not produce the necessary energy output required for rocket propulsion.
Only the combination of liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen is used as a rocket propellant. Liquid oxygen and liquid nitrogen do not form an effective propellant mixture.
The correct answer is option A, as liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen are indeed used as a rocket propellant.

questions.explanation: A rocket propellant requires a fuel and an oxidizer that react to produce a large amount of hot gas for thrust. The combination of liquid hydrogen (the fuel) and liquid oxygen (the oxidizer) is a very high-energy and efficient propellant combination.

questions.text: Very pure H2 (99.9%) can be made by which of the following processes?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This method, known as steam methane reforming, generates hydrogen but typically yields a mixture containing carbon monoxide and other contaminants, resulting in lower purity levels.

options.text: Mixing natural hydrocarbons of high molecular weight
options.explanation: This approach does not effectively produce high-purity hydrogen. The decomposition of large hydrocarbons leads to various impurities, making the hydrogen obtained less pure.
This is the correct answer because electrolysis splits pure water into hydrogen and oxygen without producing other gases, so the hydrogen obtained is extremely pure (≈99.9%).

options.text: Reaction of ionic hydrides with water
options.explanation: This is incorrect because reacting ionic hydrides with water also produces impurities (like leftover hydride, oxide or hydroxide particles), so the hydrogen is not as pure as that from electrolysis.

questions.explanation: The electrolysis of pure water (often containing a small amount of an electrolyte like NaOH or H₂SO₄ to conduct current) produces extremely pure hydrogen gas at the cathode and pure oxygen gas at the anode.

questions.text: Which of the following hydrides has the lowest boiling point?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Water (H₂O) has a high boiling point due to strong hydrogen bonding, which is much stronger than the van der Waals forces present in the other hydrides.
Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) has the lowest boiling point due to weaker intermolecular forces and a lower molecular weight compared to the other hydrides listed.
Hydrogen selenide (H₂Se) has a higher boiling point than H₂S because it has a greater molecular weight, leading to stronger van der Waals forces.
Hydrogen telluride (H₂Te) has an even higher boiling point due to its larger molecular weight, which results in stronger van der Waals forces compared to H₂S.

questions.explanation: Boiling points depend on intermolecular forces. H₂O has strong hydrogen bonding, giving it the highest boiling point. H₂S, H₂Se, and H₂Te have only van der Waals forces, which increase with molecular mass. Since H₂S is the lightest and has the weakest van der Waals forces, it has the lowest boiling point.

questions.text: Hydrogen combines directly with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hydrogen can combine directly with calcium (Ca) to form the compound calcium hydride, CaH₂. This compound is commonly used as a drying agent in chemistry laboratories due to its reactivity with water.
Hydrogen (H₂) does not combine directly with copper (Cu) under normal conditions. Copper does not form a direct hydride with hydrogen due to its relatively low reactivity.
Hydrogen (H₂) does not combine directly with zinc (Zn) under normal conditions. Zinc is a metal that does not readily react with hydrogen gas to form a compound without significant energy input.

Hydrogen does not combine directly with iron (Fe) under normal conditions to form a simple binary compound. The combination with iron typically requires specific conditions, such as in the process of hydrogenation in industrial processes.

questions.explanation: Hydrogen reacts directly with highly reactive metals, such as the alkali and most alkaline earth metals, to form ionic (saline) hydrides. It will react directly with calcium (Ca) upon heating to form calcium hydride (CaH₂).

questions.text: A substance A reacts with water, producing a combustible gas B and a solution of substance C. Substance D reacts with the solution of C and produces the same gas B. D also produces gas B on reaction with dilute H₂SO₄. A imparts a deep golden yellow colour to a flame. A, B, C, and D, respectively, are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When sodium (Na) reacts with water, it produces hydrogen gas (H₂) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Zinc (Zn) reacts with NaOH to produce hydrogen gas upon warming and also reacts with dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) to produce hydrogen gas. Sodium imparts a deep golden yellow color to a smokeless flame, aligning with the given description.

Potassium (K) reacts with water to produce hydrogen gas and potassium hydroxide (KOH). However, aluminium (Al) does not produce hydrogen gas when reacted with KOH or dilute sulfuric acid as described in the question.
Calcium (Ca) reacts with water to form calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂) and hydrogen gas. Tin (Sn) does not react with Ca(OH)₂ to produce hydrogen gas as required by the scenario.

Calcium carbide (CaC₂) reacts with water to produce acetylene gas (C₂H₂) and calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂). Acetylene is combustible, but it does not align with the described gas produced by reactions with D or dilute sulfuric acid.

questions.explanation: The golden yellow flame test identifies A as Sodium (Na). Na reacts with water to produce H₂ gas (B) and NaOH solution (C). Zinc (D) is an amphoteric metal that reacts with both NaOH and H₂SO₄ to produce H₂ gas.

questions.text: Hardness of water is due to the presence of salts of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hardness of water is actually due to the presence of bicarbonates, chlorides, and sulphates of Ca and Mg. These Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions react with the anions of fatty acids present in soaps to form curdy white precipitates, resulting in hard water not producing lather with soap immediately.

Correct! The hardness of water is indeed caused by the presence of bicarbonates, chlorides, and sulphates of Ca and Mg. These ions interact with fatty acids in soaps, leading to the formation of insoluble precipitates that reduce lather formation.
Hardness of water is primarily due to the presence of Ca2+ and Mg2+, not Ca2+ and K+.
While Ca2+ is one of the ions responsible for water hardness, the other ion is Mg2+ rather than Na+.

questions.explanation: Hardness in water is primarily caused by the presence of dissolved divalent cations, specifically calcium (Ca²⁺) and magnesium (Mg²⁺) ions. These ions react with soap to form precipitate, preventing lathering.

questions.text: Sodium carbonate reacts with SO2 in aqueous solution to give:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sodium carbonate does not react with SO2 to give NaHCO3. The correct product is Na2SO3.
Sodium carbonate does not react with SO2 to give NaHSO3. The correct product is Na2SO3.
Correct! Sodium carbonate reacts with SO2 in aqueous solution to give Na2SO3.Na2CO3 + SO2 → Na2SO3 + CO2
Sodium carbonate does not react with SO2 to give NaHSO4. The correct product is Na2SO3.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: Na2SO3. Sodium carbonate reacts with SO2 in aqueous solution to give Na2SO3. The reaction can be represented as follows: Na2CO3 + SO2 → Na2SO3 + CO2Option A, B, and D are incorrect as they do not represent the correct product formed in the given chemical reaction.

questions.text: Sodium thiosulfate, Na₂S₂O₃ ·5H₂O, is used in photography to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Reduce the silver bromide grains to metallic silver
options.explanation: Sodium thiosulfate, commonly known as hypo, is used in photography, but not to reduce silver bromide grains to metallic silver.

options.text: Convert the metallic silver to silver salt
options.explanation: Sodium thiosulfate, often called hypo, is used as a fixer in photography. It's not involved in converting metallic silver to a silver salt.

options.text: Remove undecomposed AgBr as soluble silver thiosulphate complex
options.explanation: Sodium thiosulfate, commonly known as hypo, is used in photography to remove undecomposed AgBr as a soluble silver thiosulphate complex, making this the correct answer.
Sodium thiosulfate, also known as hypo, is commonly used in photography as a fixing agent rather than a reducer.

questions.explanation: In the photographic fixing process, sodium thiosulfate (hypo) is used to remove the unexposed and undeveloped silver bromide (AgBr) from the film. It does this by forming a stable, soluble complex ion, [Ag(S₂O₃)₂]³⁻, which can then be washed away.

questions.text: Na2CO3 can be manufactured by Solvay process but K2CO3 cannot be prepared because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the solubility of K2CO3 itself is not the critical factor in the Solvay process.
This option is incorrect because the solubility of K2CO3 is not relevant to the problem at hand.

options.text: KHCO3 is more soluble than NaHCO3
options.explanation: This option is correct. The greater solubility of KHCO3 compared to NaHCO3 prevents the precipitation necessary for the Solvay process to work with potassium compounds.

options.text: KHCO3 is less soluble than NaHCO3
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because KHCO3 is actually more soluble, which is why K2CO3 cannot be prepared using the Solvay process.

questions.explanation: The Solvay process relies on the precipitation of bicarbonates. In the case of sodium, NaHCO3 is less soluble and precipitates out easily, allowing for the subsequent steps to produce Na2CO3. However, KHCO3 is more soluble and does not precipitate, making it unsuitable for the Solvay process to manufacture K2CO3. Thus, the correct answer is that KHCO3 is more soluble than NaHCO3. The other options incorrectly focus on the solubility of the carbonates themselves rather than the critical bicarbonate stage in the process.

questions.text: The blue colour of alkali and alkaline earth metals in liquid NH₃ is due to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly attributes the blue color to complex cation formation. While alkali and alkaline earth metals do form complexes in ammonia, the blue color is primarily due to solvated electrons, not cations.

This is the correct answer. The deep blue color in liquid ammonia arises from the presence of solvated electrons, which are created when the metals dissolve, and their valence electrons become surrounded by ammonia molecules.

This option is incorrect, as the blue color is not due to d–d transitions. This type of transition is characteristic of transition metals and does not apply to alkali or alkaline earth metals in ammonia.

This option is misleading. While ammoniated complex cations can form, they do not account for the blue color. The blue color is specifically due to ammoniated electrons, making this option incorrect as well.

questions.explanation: When alkali or alkaline earth metals dissolve in liquid ammonia, they release their valence electrons, which become solvated by the ammonia molecules. These "ammoniated electrons" are responsible for the solution's strong reducing properties and its characteristic deep blue color.

questions.text: Arrange the following increasing order of Hydration energyBe++, Mg++, Ca++, Ba++, Si++
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This order is incorrect because it suggests that beryllium has the lowest hydration energy, which contradicts the rule that smaller cations have higher hydration energy.
This order is incorrect because silicon is not part of the Group II elements, and beryllium should have the highest hydration energy.
This is the correct order as it reflects the relationship between the size of the cations and their hydration energy. Smaller cations have higher hydration energy.
This order is incorrect because calcium should have a higher hydration energy than magnesium due to its smaller size compared to barium.

questions.explanation: Hydration energy is inversely related to the size of the cation. Smaller cations have higher hydration energy because they can attract water molecules more strongly. Among the given cations, Be++ is the smallest, followed by Mg++, Ca++, and Ba++. Si++ is not usually considered because silicon does not commonly form a +2 cation in aqueous solution, but if included, it would be larger than Be++ and Mg++. Therefore, the correct order is Ba++ < Si++ < Ca++ < Mg++ < Be++. The other options are incorrect as they do not correctly reflect the relationship between cation size and hydration energy.

questions.text: Property of all the alkaline earth metals that increases with their atomic number is :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ionization energy generally decreases down a group in the periodic table, which means it tends to decrease as you move from top to bottom within the alkaline earth metals. This trend is primarily due to the increase in atomic size and the shielding effect of inner electrons, which makes it easier to remove outer electrons.
The solubility of alkaline earth metal hydroxides generally increases as you go down the group, but there's an interesting exception with Be(OH)2, which is amphoteric and behaves differently.

The solubility of alkaline earth metal hydroxides in water increases with their atomic number. This means that as you move down group IIA in the periodic table, the solubility of the hydroxides increases
Electronegativity generally decreases as you move down a group in the periodic table, and the alkaline earth metals are no exception. They all become less electronegative as you go down the group.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Solubility of their hydroxides. The property of alkaline earth metals that increases with their atomic number is the solubility of their hydroxides. This trend is observed as you go down the group due to the larger size of the atoms and the corresponding increase in polarizability. The other options are incorrect because ionization energy generally decreases, solubility of sulphates does not exhibit a clear trend with atomic number, and electronegativity decreases as you move down the group.

questions.text: The bleaching action of bleaching powder is due to the formation of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Calcium chloride (CaCl2) is not the correct answer. The bleaching action of bleaching powder is not due to the formation of CaCl2.
Calcium sulfate (CaSO4) is not the correct answer. The bleaching action of bleaching powder is not due to the formation of CaSO4.

Correct! The bleaching action of bleaching powder is due to the formation of hypochlorous acid (HClO). In the presence of dilute acids, bleaching powder loses oxygen, which is used for oxidation and bleaching.
Calcium chlorate (Ca(ClO3)2) is not the correct answer. The bleaching action of bleaching powder is not due to the formation of Ca(ClO3)2.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is HClO. The bleaching action of bleaching powder is due to the formation of hypochlorous acid (HClO). In the presence of dilute acids, bleaching powder loses oxygen, which is used for oxidation and bleaching. The other options (CaCl2, CaSO4, Ca(ClO3)2) are incorrect as they do not play a role in the bleaching action of bleaching powder.

questions.text: A compound ‘X’ on heating gives a colourless gas. The residue is dissolved in water to produce ‘Y’. When CO₂ is passed through the aqueous solution of ‘Y’, it produces ‘Z’, which on gentle heating gives back ‘X’. Compound ‘X’ is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not correct. Na₂CO₃ is not produced by the reaction of the compound 'X'. When the right compound reacts, it should produce a metal oxide that can be transformed back into the original compound.

questions.text: A metal X on heating in nitrogen gives Y. Y on treatment with H2O gives a colourless gas which when passed through sodium hypochlorite liberates N2 gas. The compound Y is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Magnesium nitrate does not form by heating magnesium in nitrogen. Instead, it is a nitrate salt, which does not react with water to produce ammonia.
Magnesium nitride forms when magnesium is heated in nitrogen. It reacts with water to produce ammonia, which releases nitrogen gas when treated with sodium hypochlorite.
Ammonia is a product of the reaction between magnesium nitride and water, not a direct product of heating metal X in nitrogen.
Magnesium oxide does not form by heating magnesium in nitrogen but rather in the presence of oxygen. It does not react with water to produce ammonia.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Mg3N2. When magnesium (X) is heated in nitrogen, it forms magnesium nitride (Y). The chemical reaction is as follows: 3Mg + N2 → Mg3N2. When magnesium nitride (Y) is treated with water, it reacts to produce magnesium hydroxide and ammonia: Mg3N2 + 6H2O → 3Mg(OH)2 + 2NH3. The ammonia, being a colorless gas, reacts with sodium hypochlorite to release nitrogen gas: 2NH3 + 3NaClO → N2 + 3NaCl + 3H2O. Other options, such as magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), ammonia (NH3), and magnesium oxide (MgO), do not fit the series of reactions described in the question.

questions.text: The hydroxide which is best soluble in water is:

Barium hydroxide, being the largest cation, has the highest solubility in water due to weaker ion-dipole interactions.

Magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2) is less soluble in water compared to barium hydroxide. As you move down the group, the solubility of Group 2A hydroxides increases, making magnesium hydroxide less soluble than barium hydroxide.

Strontium hydroxide (Sr(OH)2) is more soluble in water compared to magnesium hydroxide but less soluble than barium hydroxide. The trend of increasing solubility as you move down the group is evident here as well.

Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) is less soluble in water compared to strontium and barium hydroxides. The trend of increasing solubility down the group makes calcium hydroxide less soluble than the other options.

questions.explanation: Barium hydroxide (Ba(OH)2) is the correct answer. The solubility trend of hydroxides of Group 2A metals increases as you move down the group due to the larger size of the cation and weaker ion-dipole interactions. This results in barium hydroxide being the most soluble among the options provided. Magnesium hydroxide, strontium hydroxide, and calcium hydroxide are less soluble in water compared to barium hydroxide.

questions.text: Magnesium on reaction with (very dilute) HNO3 gives:

When magnesium reacts with very dilute nitric acid, it forms magnesium nitrate and hydrogen gas is evolved.As the nitric acid is very dilute, it acts as a weak oxidising agent, which is unable to oxidise the hydrogen gas.Mg(s) + 2HNO3(aq) → Mg(NO3)2(aq) + H2(g)HNO3 is a very strong oxidising agent. On reacting with other metals, it oxidises their outer layer and renders them passive(unreactive)HNO3 (about 1% concentrated) doesn’t oxidise Mg and Mn a lot and so it reacts with them as you would expect an acid to react, releasing hydrogen.Technically, metal + acid ----> metal salt +water.But when we look at HNO3, it has a tendency to form H2O and not H2 when it reacts with metals.But, Magnesium and Manganese are two metals that traditionally liberate H2 and not H2O when they react with HNO3. This is because, when they initially react, they do form H2O, but the Mg or Mn metals are strong reducing agents and hence they reduce H2O and themselves oxidise. This leaves them with only H2 gas.Hence, when Mg/Mn reacts with Nitric acid to form a salt and H2O, but the H2O is quickly (almost instantaneously) reduced and converted to H2 gas.
Mg + 2HNO3 → (very dilute) Mg(NO3)2 + H2 Thus NO2 is not formed.

questions.explanation: When magnesium reacts with very dilute nitric acid, it forms magnesium nitrate and hydrogen gas is evolved. options.text: BeF2 forms a complex compound with excess NaF, in which the complex entity containing Be, is a cation.
options.explanation: Beryllium fluoride (BeF2) forms a complex compound with excess sodium fluoride (NaF), in which the complex entity containing Be is an anion. The complex is called sodium tetrafluoroberyllate (Na2[BeF4]2-). The formation of the complex can be represented by the following equation:BeF2 + 2NaF → Na2[BeF4]2- In the complex, the beryllium ion (Be2+) is surrounded by four fluoride ions (F-). The beryllium ion has a charge of +2, while each fluoride ion has a charge of -1. The overall charge of the complex is -2. The complex is an anion because it has a negative charge. The negative charge is due to the presence of the four fluoride ions. The fluoride ions are attracted to the beryllium ion because of the opposite charges.

Covalent halides of aluminum (Al) and beryllium (Be) are compounds in which these elements form covalent bonds with halogens (Group 17 elements) such as fluorine (F), chlorine (Cl), bromine (Br), and iodine (I). These compounds have varying properties and can be used in different applications.

Due to diagonal relationship, Be and Al exhibit similar properties. However, the covalency of Be is 4 because it lacks d-orbitals, whereas the covalency of Al can go up to 6 due to the presence of vacant d-orbitals. Therefore, they differ in exhibiting the maximum covalency in compounds.Maximum covalence of Be is 4 while that of Al is 6. This is due to the unavailability of d-orbitals in the 2nd shell (valence shell of Be).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that Be and Al differ in exhibiting the maximum covalency in compounds. Beryllium (Be) has a maximum covalency of 4 due to the absence of d-orbitals, while aluminum (Al) can exhibit a covalency of up to 6 because it has vacant d-orbitals. This difference is a result of their electronic configurations. The other options are incorrect because they do not address this specific difference between Be and Al in terms of covalency in compounds.

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Cu2O is not a component of the slag formed during the smelting of copper. The slag primarily consists of iron silicate, not copper compounds.
This is the correct answer. During the smelting process, iron in the form of iron sulfide (FeS) reacts with silica (SiO2) to form ferrous silicate (FeSiO3), which is the slag.
This option is incorrect. CuFeS2 is chalcopyrite, the primary ore of copper, and not part of the slag.
This option is incorrect. Cu2S is one of the products of copper ore reduction and FeO does not form the slag in this reaction.

questions.explanation: In the extraction of copper from its ores, iron is a common impurity that needs to be removed during the smelting process. This is achieved by adding silica (SiO2) to the smelt, which reacts with iron sulfide (FeS) present in the ore. The reaction between FeS and SiO2 forms ferrous silicate (FeSiO3), which is the slag that is removed. This slag formation helps in purifying the copper by separating out the iron impurities. Other options, such as Cu2O, CuFeS2, and Cu2S + FeO, do not accurately describe the composition of the slag or are not relevant to the slag formation process.

questions.text: How is limestone used in Fe extraction :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Limestone is not used in the direct oxidation of iron ore. Oxidation of iron ore typically involves the introduction of oxygen or an oxidizing agent to convert iron compounds into their oxide forms.

This option is incorrect. Limestone is not used in the reduction of iron ore. Reduction of iron ore involves the removal of oxygen to convert iron oxide (Fe2O3) into iron (Fe) through a reduction reaction, often using carbon monoxide (CO) or hydrogen (H2) as reducing agents.

This option is correct. Limestone is used in the formation of slag during the extraction of iron from its ore in a blast furnace. The blast furnace process involves heating iron ore (typically hematite or magnetite) along with coke (a form of carbon) and fluxes like limestone. Limestone acts as a flux by combining with impurities in the ore, such as silica (SiO2), to form a slag. The slag floats on top of the molten iron and is easily separated, carrying away the impurities. This helps in the purification of iron.

This option is incorrect. Limestone is not used for the direct purification of iron formed in the extraction process. The purification of iron typically involves further refining processes after it has been extracted from the ore.

questions.text: Which method of purification is represented by the equation :(impure)Ti + 2I2 →500k TiI4 → 1675k Ti + 2I2 pure
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a method used for refining noble metals like gold and silver. It involves heating the impure metal in the presence of lead, which forms a litharge (lead oxide) slag that carries away impurities. This process is not relevant to titanium purification.
Poling is a method used to remove impurities like sulfur from molten metals like copper by adding a material that reacts with the impurities. This process is not relevant to titanium purification.

CorrectThe equation you've given involves the purification of titanium (Ti) using the Van Arkel process, which is also sometimes referred to as the iodide process. In this process, an impure sample of titanium is reacted with iodine (I2) to form titanium tetraiodide (TiI4) at a relatively low temperature (500 K). The titanium tetraiodide is then heated to a higher temperature (1675 K), causing it to decompose back into titanium and iodine. The titanium produced is now in a purified form, as the impurities remain in the titanium tetraiodide and are separated from the final product.

Zone refining is a purification technique primarily used for semiconductor materials. It involves passing a molten zone through a solid material, causing impurities to move along with the zone. This process is not commonly used for titanium purification.

The titanium produced is now in a purified form, as the impurities remain in the titanium tetraiodide and are separated from the final product.

questions.explanation: While coke (carbon) is added to the furnace, it primarily burns to produce carbon monoxide (CO). This carbon monoxide gas then acts as the main chemical reducing agent, removing oxygen from the iron oxides to produce molten iron.

questions.text: Froth floatation process for the concentration of ores is a practical application of :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CorrectFroth flotation is a widely used process for separating valuable minerals from gangue (unwanted material) in ore minerals. It relies on the differences in the surface properties of minerals to selectively separate them. Here's how adsorption applies to the process:Collectors and Frothers: In froth flotation, chemicals called collectors are used to selectively bind to the surface of the target minerals, making them hydrophobic (water-repellent). Frothers are added to create a stable froth on the surface of the flotation cell.Formation of Froth: The mineral-laden mixture is aerated, and air bubbles attach to the hydrophobic mineral particles, carrying them to the surface of the flotation cell to form a froth layer.Selective Separation: The froth, containing the hydrophobic minerals, is skimmed off, while the hydrophilic (water-attracting) gangue minerals sink and are discarded. This results in the selective separation of valuable minerals from the ore.Adsorption: The success of froth flotation relies on the adsorption of collectors on the surface of the mineral particles. Collectors adsorb preferentially onto the mineral surfaces, changing their surface properties and allowing them to attach to air bubbles in the flotation cell.

IncorrectAbsorption involves one substance being taken up or absorbed by another substance. In froth flotation, it's not absorption but adsorption that plays a crucial role. The collectors adsorb onto the mineral surfaces.

Incorrect Coagulation is the process of particles coming together to form larger particles, called flocs. While coagulation might be involved in some separation processes, it is not the primary mechanism of froth flotation.

Incorrect Sedimentation involves the settling of solid particles from a liquid due to gravity. In froth flotation, the process relies on the opposite action—bubbles carrying hydrophobic particles to the surface—rather than the settling of particles.

questions.text: Complex formation or cyanide method is used for the extraction of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is correct. Copper is extracted from its ores by the cyanide process. In this process, the ore is crushed and then treated with a solution of sodium cyanide (NaCN). The copper in the ore reacts with the sodium cyanide to form a soluble complex called copper cyanide (CuCN). The copper cyanide is then removed from the solution by precipitation, while the other metals in the ore remain in the solution. The precipitation of copper cyanide can be carried out by adding a solution of zinc sulfate (ZnSO4). The zinc sulfate reacts with the copper cyanide to form a precipitate of copper sulfate (CuSO4).The copper sulfate is then recovered from the precipitate and then reduced to copper metal by heating it in the presence of carbon.
The cyanide method is not used for the extraction of Fe. Iron is extracted from its ores by the blast furnace process.

Mercury is extracted from its ores by the amalgamation process. In this process, the ore is crushed and then treated with mercury. The mercury in the ore reacts with the copper in the ore to form an amalgam. The amalgam is then separated from the ore and then distilled to recover the mercury.
The cyanide method is not used for the extraction of F. Fluorine is extracted from its ores by the electrolysis process.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because copper is indeed extracted from its ores using the cyanide process. The other options are incorrect because they do not involve the cyanide method for extraction. Option B mentions iron (Fe), which is extracted by the blast furnace process. Option C refers to mercury (Hg), which is extracted by the amalgamation process. Option D discusses fluorine (F), which is extracted by electrolysis, not the cyanide method.

questions.text: Mixture of chlorides of copper, cadmium, chromium iron and aluminium was dissolved in water acidified with HCl and H2S gas was passed for a sufficient time. It was filtered boiled and a few drops of nitric acid were added while boiling. To this solution ammonium chloride and sodium hydroxide were added and filtered. The filtrate would give test for:

On passing H2S in the presence of HCl, Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions are precipitated. The addition of HNO3, NH4Cl, and NaOH to the filtrate results in the formation of Na2AlO2, which confirms the presence of sodium and aluminum ions. Therefore, this option is correct.
While sodium and aluminum are present, iron is not present in the filtrate since it precipitated as iron sulfide. Thus, this option is incorrect.
Cadmium and iron are precipitated out during the process, so they do not remain in the filtrate. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The addition of HNO3, NH4Cl, and NaOH to the boiled filtrate leads to the formation of Na2AlO2, which confirms the presence of sodium and aluminum ions in the solution. questions.text: The chemical processes in the production of steel from hematite ore involve :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect option. Reduction only involves addition of hydrogen.
Incorrect option. Oxidation only involves addition of oxygen.
Incorrect option. Please refer to the main explanation for the correct order of processes.

The chemical processes in the production of steel from hematite ore involve oxidation followed by reduction. The first step in the process is to oxidize the iron ore to form iron oxide. This is done by heating the ore in the presence of oxygen. The reaction is given by the following equation: 2Fe2O3 + 3C → 4Fe + 3CO2 In this reaction, the iron oxide is oxidized to iron, while the carbon is reduced to carbon dioxide. The second step in the process is to reduce the iron oxide to iron. This is done by heating the iron oxide in the presence of carbon monoxide. The reaction is given by the following equation: Fe2O3 + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO2 In this reaction, the iron oxide is reduced to iron, while the carbon monoxide is oxidized to carbon dioxide. The overall process of producing steel from hematite ore is a two-step process: Oxidation of the iron ore to form iron oxide. Reduction of the iron oxide to iron. The oxidation step is carried out in a blast furnace, while the reduction step is carried out in a steelmaking furnace.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. The chemical processes in the production of steel from hematite ore involve oxidation followed by reduction. The first step in the process is to oxidize the iron ore to form iron oxide. This is done by heating the ore in the presence of oxygen. The reaction is given by the following equation: 2Fe2O3 + 3C → 4Fe + 3CO2 In this reaction, the iron oxide is oxidized to iron, while the carbon is reduced to carbon dioxide. The second step in the process is to reduce the iron oxide to iron. This is done by heating the iron oxide in the presence of carbon monoxide. The reaction is given by the following equation: Fe2O3 + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO2 In this reaction, the iron oxide is reduced to iron, while the carbon monoxide is oxidized to carbon dioxide. The overall process of producing steel from hematite ore is a two-step process: Oxidation of the iron ore to form iron oxide. Reduction of the iron oxide to iron. The oxidation step is carried out in a blast furnace, while the reduction step is carried out in a steelmaking furnace.

questions.text: In the Hoope’s process for refining of aluminium, the fused materials form three different layers and they remain separated during electrolysis also. This is because:

options.text: There is special arrangement in the cell to keep the layers separate.
options.explanation: The specific gravity of the different materials is different. The specific gravity of pure aluminium is 2.7 g/cm3, the specific gravity of cryolite is 2.2 g/cm3, and the specific gravity of the impurities is 3 g/cm3. This means that the pure aluminium will float on the cryolite, and the impurities will sink to the bottom.

options.text: The three layers have different densities.
options.explanation: The three layers have different densities. In the Hoope's process for refining of aluminium, the fused materials form three different layers: Upper layer: This layer is composed of pure aluminium. It has the highest density and floats on the surface of the electrolyte.Middle layer: This layer is composed of a mixture of aluminium oxide and aluminium. It has a lower density than the upper layer but higher than the lower layer. Lower layer: This layer is composed of aluminium oxide and other impurities. It has the lowest density and sinks to the bottom of the electrolyte.

options.text: The upper layer is kept attracted by the cathode and the lower layer is kept attracted by the anode.
options.explanation: Electrolysis: The impure aluminium is melted and electrolyzed in a bath of cryolite. The aluminium ions in the electrolyte are attracted to the cathode, where they are reduced to pure aluminium metal. The impurities in the electrolyte are attracted to the anode, where they are oxidized and removed from the electrolyte.
Correct option.

questions.text: Among the following separation processes, concentration technique is used in the-----
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Calcination is the process of heating a material to a high temperature in the absence of air. This process is used to remove water and other volatile impurities from the material.

Froth floatation is a process used to concentrate minerals that are different in their wettability. In this process, the ore is suspended in water and a frothing agent is added. The frothing agent causes the mineral particles to be attracted to the air bubbles and float to the top of the mixture. The other mineral particles sink to the bottom and are discarded.

Leaching is a process used to dissolve a desired metal from an ore. In this process, the ore is crushed and mixed with a solvent that can dissolve the desired metal. The mixture is then agitated and the dissolved metal is extracted from the solvent.
Correct option.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. Froth floatation and Leaching are both concentration techniques used in the separation of metals. Froth floatation concentrates minerals based on their wettability, while leaching dissolves the desired metal from an ore. Calcination, on the other hand, is a process used to remove impurities through high-temperature heating, but it is not a concentration technique in metal separation.

questions.text: In which group melting and boiling point decreases down the group:

As the atomic radius increases, the delocalized electrons are further away from the nuclei and the attractive force is weaker.
The melting and boiling points of the halogens increase as you go down the group. This is because the van der Waals forces between the molecules increase as the molecules get larger.
The noble gases do not have any melting or boiling points at standard temperature and pressure. This is because they are non-polar and do not have any intermolecular forces.

The melting and boiling points of the elements in the nitrogen group do not have a clear trend. The melting and boiling points of nitrogen and phosphorus decrease as you go down the group, but the melting and boiling points of arsenic and antimony increase.

questions.text: The alkali metals are highly reactive elements. The reactivity of alkali metals is due to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from a neutral atom in the gaseous state. Alkali metals have low ionization energy because they have one valence electron in their outermost energy level, which is shielded from the nucleus by inner electron layers. This valence electron is held weakly by the positively charged nucleus, making it relatively easy to remove. When this valence electron is removed, the atom becomes a positively charged ion, which is highly reactive and eager to participate in chemical reactions.

Enthalpy of atomization is the energy required to convert one mole of a substance in the gaseous state into individual atoms. Alkali metals have low enthalpy of atomization because the metallic bonds holding the atoms together in their solid state are relatively weak. This means that it takes relatively little energy to break these bonds and separate the atoms into their individual, reactive forms.

The reactivity of alkali metals is a result of both their low ionization energy and low enthalpy of atomization. The low ionization energy allows alkali metals to easily lose their valence electron, becoming positively charged ions. Simultaneously, their low enthalpy of atomization means that the metal atoms can easily break apart into individual atoms. Both of these factors contribute to the high reactivity of alkali metals, making option (c) the correct answer.
This option is incorrect as both options A and B are correct.

N2O5 is an acid oxide. An acidic oxide reacts with water to form an acid. N2O5 is an acidic oxide because it reacts with water to produce nitric acid (HNO3).
An intermediate hydride like BeH2 is a compound that falls between ionic and covalent hydrides in terms of its properties.

questions.explanation: N2O5 is an acidic oxide, not a basic oxide. An acidic oxide reacts with water to form an acid. N2O5 is an acidic oxide because it reacts with water to produce nitric acid (HNO3). BaO, Ga2O3, and BeH2 are correctly matched with their oxide types, making Option C the correct answer.

questions.text: Which of the following oxides of an element is strongly basic in nature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sodium oxide (Na2O) is an ionic compound composed of sodium (Na) and oxygen (O). It dissociates in water to form sodium ions (Na+) and oxide ions (O2-), which can react with water to form hydroxide ions (OH-), making the solution strongly basic.
Magnesium oxide (MgO) is basic, but not strongly basic.
Aluminum oxide (Al2O3) is amphoteric, exhibiting both acidic and basic properties.
Sulfur trioxide (SO3) is acidic in nature.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because sodium oxide (Na2O) dissociates in water to form hydroxide ions, making the solution strongly basic. Option B (MgO) is basic but not strongly basic. Option C (Al2O3) is amphoteric, and Option D (SO3) is acidic. Therefore, the correct answer is sodium oxide (Na2O), as it exhibits strong basic properties.

questions.text: Beryllium element belongs to the period:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Beryllium is located in period 2 of the periodic table, indicating it has two electron shells. This makes Option A the correct choice.
Although close, Beryllium actually belongs to the second period, not the third. Therefore, Option B is incorrect.
Beryllium is in the second period, not the fourth. This makes Option C incorrect.
Even though it seems plausible, Beryllium does not belong to the fifth period. Thus, Option D is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because beryllium is located in period 2 of the periodic table. The periods in the periodic table represent the energy levels or shells in which the electrons of an element are arranged. Beryllium has two electron shells, so it belongs to period 2. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they refer to periods that do not correspond to the placement of Beryllium in the periodic table.

questions.text: Which of the following is not amphoteric oxide?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Al2O3 is indeed an amphoteric oxide, meaning it can react with both acids and bases.
ZnO is an amphoteric oxide, capable of reacting with both acids and bases.
CaO is not amphoteric; it is a basic oxide that reacts with acids. The correct answer is CaO.
PbO is an amphoteric oxide, capable of reacting with both acids and bases.

questions.explanation: CaO (Calcium oxide) is not amphoteric; it is a basic oxide that reacts with acids. Lead(II) oxide (PbO) is the amphoteric oxide in this question. Al2O3 and ZnO are also amphoteric oxides, meaning they can react with both acids and bases.

questions.text: Which of the following is semiconductor
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aluminium is a metal known for its high electrical conductivity, making it a good conductor but not a semiconductor.
Diamond is an excellent insulator, not capable of being a semiconductor due to its lack of electrical conductivity.
Graphite is a good conductor of electricity but does not exhibit semiconductor properties required for this question.
Silicon is a well-known semiconductor extensively used in electronic devices and computer chips due to its unique conductivity characteristics.

questions.explanation: Silicon is the correct answer as it is a semiconductor with properties that allow it to be used in electronic devices. Semiconductors like silicon have conductivity levels between conductors and insulators, making them crucial for technology. Aluminium is a good conductor but not a semiconductor. Diamond is an insulator, lacking the conductivity needed for semiconductor functions. Graphite, although conductive, does not possess the specific semiconductor properties required for this question.

questions.text: Diamond is hard because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It has a high value of refractive index
options.explanation: While diamond does have a high refractive index, this property is related to its optical characteristics, not its hardness.

options.text: All the valence electrons of each carbon atom are involved in bonding
options.explanation: This is true for diamond, as each carbon atom forms four covalent bonds. However, it does not directly explain the hardness of diamond.

options.text: It is a giant covalent structure
options.explanation: Diamond is indeed a giant covalent structure, where each carbon atom is tetrahedrally bonded to four others, creating a very strong and rigid three-dimensional network. This is what makes diamond extremely hard.

options.text: It is a crystalline allotrope of carbon
options.explanation: While diamond is a crystalline allotrope of carbon, this fact alone does not explain its hardness. The key lies in its covalent bonding and structure.

questions.explanation: Diamond is a form of carbon with a giant covalent structure, where each carbon atom forms four strong covalent bonds, creating a rigid, three-dimensional network that results in exceptional hardness. Option C is correct as it emphasizes the importance of the giant covalent structure. Option A is incorrect because the refractive index relates to optical properties, not hardness. Option B, although true for diamond, does not directly explain its hardness. Option D only describes diamond's allotropy and crystalline nature, which do not fully explain its hardness.

questions.text: A gas which burns with a blue flame is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CO₂ is a non-flammable gas used in fire extinguishers and does not burn with a blue flame.
NO is a reactive gas but it does not typically burn with a blue flame.
N₂ is an inert gas under standard conditions and does not burn with a blue flame.
CO (carbon monoxide) is a flammable gas that burns in the presence of oxygen with a characteristic blue flame.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: CO. Carbon monoxide (CO) is a flammable gas that, when burned in oxygen, produces a blue flame due to its exothermic reaction. It is commonly known for its use in gas stoves and heaters.Option A: CO₂ is incorrect because carbon dioxide is a non-flammable gas that is actually used to extinguish fires.Option B: NO is incorrect as nitric oxide is not known for burning with a blue flame.Option C: N₂ is incorrect because nitrogen is an inert gas that does not burn under normal conditions.

questions.text: Which of the following shows inert pair effect
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Boron is not typically associated with the inert pair effect as it is not an s-block element.
Carbon is also not an s-block element and does not exhibit the inert pair effect.
Silicon, being a p-block element, does not demonstrate the inert pair effect observed in s-block elements.

Tin is an s-block element that shows the inert pair effect by preferentially losing its outermost s electrons rather than participating in chemical bonding. This phenomenon leads to its variable oxidation states and chemical reactivity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D, Tin. The inert pair effect is a tendency of the heavier s-block elements to preferentially lose their outermost s electrons rather than participate in chemical reactions. Boron, Carbon, and Silicon are not s-block elements and thus do not exhibit the inert pair effect. In the case of Tin, it shows this effect by retaining its 2 outermost s electrons in certain compounds, leading to its variable oxidation states and reactivity.

questions.text: In a network of silica (SiO₂), each silicon atom is surrounded by _ atoms of oxygen
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In silica (SiO₂), each silicon atom forms a tetrahedral structure with four oxygen atoms, each of which bridges to another silicon atom.

Incorrect as silicon in silica is surrounded by more than one oxygen atom in a tetrahedral configuration.
This answer corresponds to an octahedral coordination, which is not observed for silicon in SiO₂.

questions.explanation: In the network structure of silica (SiO₂), each silicon atom is covalently bonded to four oxygen atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, forming a continuous three-dimensional network. Each oxygen atom is shared between two silicon atoms. Option B (2) is incorrect as it refers to a linear coordination. Option C (1) is incorrect because silicon is not surrounded by only one oxygen atom. Option D (6) suggests an octahedral coordination, which does not apply to silicon in SiO₂.

questions.text: CO₂ is gas while SiO₂ is solid at room temperature
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Carbon is a non-metal while silicon is a semi-metal
options.explanation: This option is not directly related to the physical states of CO₂ and SiO₂. The key factor determining their states is the molecular structure, not the elemental nature of carbon and silicon.

options.text: CO₂ is an independent molecule while SiO₂ has a network covalent structure
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. CO₂ exists as separate molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, making it a gas. SiO₂ forms a continuous network of covalent bonds, leading to its solid state at room temperature.

options.text: Carbon forms multiple bonds while silicon does not form multiple bonds
options.explanation: This option is partially relevant as carbon can indeed form multiple bonds. However, the key difference between CO₂ and SiO₂ lies in their molecular structures rather than the types of bonds they form.

While SiO₂ primarily consists of sigma bonds, this description does not fully explain why SiO₂ is a solid at room temperature. The extensive covalent network structure of SiO₂ is the critical factor determining its state.

questions.explanation: The physical states of CO₂ and SiO₂ at room temperature are primarily determined by their molecular structures. CO₂ is a gas because it consists of independent molecules held together by weak forces. On the other hand, SiO₂ is a solid due to its network covalent structure where silicon atoms are bonded to oxygen atoms in a 3D network. This network covalent bonding is the reason for SiO₂'s solid state. Option B is correct as it highlights this key difference in molecular structure. Option A is incorrect as the elemental nature of carbon and silicon is not directly related to their states. Option C is partially relevant but doesn't address the primary factor of molecular structure. Option D focuses on bond types rather than the network structure of SiO₂.

questions.text: Which pair of elements form acidic oxides
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Li and Be are metals that do not form acidic oxides. Lithium forms a basic oxide, while beryllium forms an amphoteric oxide.
Mg and Ca are alkaline earth metals that do not form acidic oxides. They typically form basic oxides.
Na and K are alkali metals that do not form acidic oxides. They tend to form basic oxides.
C and Si form acidic oxides. Carbon forms carbon dioxide (CO2) and silicon forms silicon dioxide (SiO2), both of which exhibit acidic properties.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: C and Si. Both carbon and silicon are non-metals that form acidic oxides. Carbon dioxide (CO2) and silicon dioxide (SiO2) are acidic oxides that react with water to form acids.Option A: Li and Be are metals that do not form acidic oxides. Lithium forms a basic oxide, while beryllium forms an amphoteric oxide.Option B: Mg and Ca are alkaline earth metals that form basic oxides, not acidic ones.Option C: Na and K are alkali metals that form basic oxides, typical of their group.

questions.text: Identify the correct statement regarding CO
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It combines with H₂O to form carbonic acid
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide (CO) does not react with water to form carbonic acid; this is a property of carbon dioxide (CO₂), not CO.

options.text: It is a slightly polar molecule
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide is a slightly polar molecule due to the difference in electronegativity between carbon and oxygen, and the presence of a triple bond with a lone pair on the carbon atom.

options.text: It is a powerful oxidizing agent
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide is not a powerful oxidizing agent; it is typically a reducing agent, particularly in metallurgical processes.

options.text: It is used to prepare aerated drinks
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide is not used to prepare aerated drinks; carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the gas used for carbonation in beverages.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that carbon monoxide (CO) is a slightly polar molecule. This is due to the polar covalent bond formed between carbon and oxygen, which results in a small dipole moment. The other options are incorrect: CO does not react with water to form carbonic acid; that is the behavior of CO₂. CO is not a powerful oxidizing agent; it is known for being a reducing agent. Lastly, CO is not used in aerated drinks; CO₂ is the gas used for carbonation in beverages.

questions.text: Which of the following is metasilicic acid
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct formula for metasilicic acid, formed from orthosilicic acid by losing one water molecule.

Incorrect. This is sulfuric acid, not related to silicic acids, as it contains sulfur, not silicon.

questions.text: The geometry around each Si-atom in SiO₂ is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Trigonal geometry involves three atoms surrounding a central atom, which is not the case in SiO₂.
Cubic geometry is observed in structures with a cubic lattice, which is not applicable to the molecular structure of SiO₂.
Linear geometry involves two atoms bonded to a central atom with a 180° angle, which does not fit the structure of SiO₂.
In SiO₂, each silicon atom is covalently bonded to four oxygen atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, making this the correct geometry.

questions.explanation: The correct geometry around each Si-atom in SiO₂ is tetrahedral. This is because silicon typically forms four covalent bonds, and in SiO₂, each silicon atom is bonded to four oxygen atoms forming a three-dimensional network. The tetrahedral arrangement minimizes electron pair repulsion, which is consistent with VSEPR theory. The other options are incorrect as they do not match the bonding and spatial arrangement around silicon in SiO₂.

questions.text: The empirical formula of quartz, opal and sand is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This represents a silicate ion, which is not the empirical formula for quartz, opal, or sand.

questions.text: Silicones differ from silica by a group of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct! Silicones have organic groups (-R) attached to silicon atoms, unlike silica. These organic groups differentiate silicones from silica in terms of structure.
Incorrect. The presence of hydroxyl groups (-OH) is not the defining difference between silicones and silica. Silicones do not contain hydroxyl groups as a distinguishing feature.
Incorrect. Bicarbonate (HCO3⁻) is not relevant to the structural comparison of silicones and silica. This compound is not associated with either silicones or silica.
Incorrect. Oxygen gas (O₂) does not play a role in differentiating silicones from silica. The presence of oxygen alone is not a distinguishing factor between silicones and silica.

questions.explanation: Silicones are polymeric compounds in which silicon atoms are bonded to organic groups, typically alkyl or aryl groups, denoted as -R. This structural feature sets silicones apart from silica, which lacks organic attachments and consists of silicon and oxygen in a network structure. Therefore, the correct answer is -R as it represents the organic groups present in silicones. The other options -OH, HCO3⁻, and O₂ are incorrect because they do not accurately reflect the structural distinction between silicones and silica.

questions.text: Silicon react with steam at red hot to produce
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct. Silicon reacts with steam at high temperatures to produce silicon dioxide (SiO₂) and hydrogen gas (H₂).
Incorrect. Silicon hydroxide (Si(OH)₄) is not the product of this reaction; silicon dioxide and hydrogen gas are formed instead.

questions.explanation: When silicon is exposed to steam at red-hot temperatures, it forms silicon dioxide (SiO₂) and releases hydrogen gas (H₂) as a byproduct. This reaction is typical for non-metals reacting with steam. Option A is correct because it aligns with the expected products. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they propose either incorrect products like silicon hydroxide or silane, or incorrect reactants like oxygen gas instead of steam.

questions.text: Indicate the hydroxide which is acid
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Silicon hydroxide, also known as orthosilicic acid, is acidic in nature as it can donate protons, making it the correct answer.
Aluminum hydroxide is amphoteric, meaning it can act as both an acid and a base, but it is not predominantly acidic.
Magnesium hydroxide is a basic hydroxide commonly used as an antacid due to its ability to neutralize acids.
Calcium hydroxide is a strong base used in various applications such as in the production of cement and as an antacid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Si(OH)₄. Silicon hydroxide behaves as an acid due to its structure that allows it to release protons. Aluminum hydroxide is amphoteric, meaning it can act as both an acid and a base, but it is not predominantly acidic. Magnesium hydroxide and calcium hydroxide are basic hydroxides that do not readily release protons, functioning as bases by accepting them instead.

questions.text: Tetrachloride of which element doesn't react with water
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) is a stable compound that does not hydrolyze in water because it forms a covalent bond with chlorine, making it non-polar and unreactive with water.
Silicon tetrachloride (SiCl4) reacts vigorously with water, forming silicic acid and hydrochloric acid. This is due to silicon's ability to form strong bonds with oxygen.
Tin tetrachloride (SnCl4) also reacts with water, producing tin dioxide and hydrochloric acid, because tin forms stable oxides.
Lead tetrachloride (PbCl4) is unstable and reacts with water to form lead dioxide and hydrochloric acid. The instabilities in the oxidation states of lead contribute to its reactivity with water.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Carbon. Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) is known for its lack of reactivity with water because it is non-polar and lacks lone pairs of electrons that could participate in hydrolysis. In contrast, Silicon, Tin, and Lead tetrachlorides can react with water. Silicon tetrachloride reacts to form silicic acid and hydrochloric acid, due to silicon's affinity for oxygen. Tin tetrachloride forms tin dioxide and hydrochloric acid, and Lead tetrachloride hydrolyzes to give lead dioxide and hydrochloric acid due to the stability of their oxide forms and the presence of available d-orbitals for bond formation.

questions.text: Which one react most violently with cold water
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: PbCl₄ is a stable compound and does not react violently with cold water.
CCl₄ is inert and does not react with water, including cold water.
SiCl₄ reacts violently with cold water, forming hydrochloric acid and silicic acid in the process.
GeCl₄ reacts with water but not as vigorously as SiCl₄ does.

questions.explanation: Among the options provided, SiCl₄ reacts most violently with cold water. When SiCl₄ comes into contact with water, it rapidly hydrolyzes to produce silicic acid (H₄SiO₄) and hydrochloric acid (HCl), releasing a significant amount of heat in the process. This reaction is more vigorous compared to the other compounds listed. PbCl₄ is stable and does not react violently with water, CCl₄ is inert, and while GeCl₄ does react with water, it is not as reactive as SiCl₄.

questions.text: Which of the following is acidic oxide

questions.text: Alkali metal exist in form of
questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.text: Consider the following general characteristics of alkali metal down the groups:I. The atomic and ionic radii increase from Li to Cs with the increasing atomic number due to the addition of new shells. II. Hardness of alkali metals decreases from Li to Cs with decreasing interatomic interactions.III. Melting and boiling points generally decrease from Li to Cs with the decreasing interatomic interactions.IV. Electropositive character increases from Li to Cs with decreasing I.E values.Which of the above statement is correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: All of the statements are correct.
All of the statements are correct.
All of the statements are correct.

All of the statements are correct. The atomic and ionic radii increase from Li to Cs with the increasing atomic number due to the addition of new shells. This is because as the atomic number increases, the electrons are added to shells that are further away from the nucleus. This makes the electrons more loosely held and the atom or ion larger. Hardness of alkali metals decreases from Li to Cs with decreasing interatomic interactions. This is because as the atoms get larger, the interatomic interactions become weaker. This makes the atoms less able to resist deformation and the metal less hard. Melting and boiling points generally decrease from Li to Cs with the decreasing interatomic interactions. This is because the melting and boiling points are a measure of the strength of the interatomic interactions. As the interatomic interactions decrease, the melting and boiling points also decrease. Electropositive character increases from Li to Cs with decreasing I.E values. This is because the electropositive character is a measure of how easily an atom loses electrons. As the ionization energy decreases, the atom becomes easier to ionize and the electropositive character increases.

This is because larger atoms have weaker metallic bonds and weaker interatomic forces compared to smaller atoms. The distance between metal ions increases with larger atomic size, leading to a reduction in the strength of metallic bonds. Consequently, less energy is required to overcome these bonds, resulting in a lower boiling point. However, exceptions to this trend can occur based on other factors, such as electron configuration or additional intermolecular forces that might be present in certain metals. Option A is incorrect because an increase in atomic size does not strengthen metallic bonds. Option C is incorrect as the boiling point is not constant; it decreases with increasing atomic size. Option D is incorrect as the question specifically asks about the relationship between atomic size and boiling point.

questions.text: Which is a conductor?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Diamond has a three-dimensional tetrahedral structure where each carbon atom is covalently bonded to four other carbon atoms. This strong bonding network does not allow for the free movement of electrons, making diamond an electrical insulator.

Graphite consists of layers of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. The electrons within these layers are delocalized, allowing them to move freely, which makes graphite an excellent conductor of electricity.
Phosphorus, in its most common forms (white, red, and black phosphorus), is a non-metal and does not have the free electrons necessary to conduct electricity effectively.
This option suggests that both graphite (Option B) and phosphorus (Option C) are conductors. However, only graphite is a good conductor of electricity, while phosphorus is not.

questions.explanation: Graphite is the only conductor in the given options. The structure of graphite allows electrons to move freely within its layers, making it an effective conductor of electricity. In contrast, diamond, despite being made of carbon like graphite, has a rigid three-dimensional network that lacks free electrons, thus acting as an insulator. Phosphorus, being a non-metal, lacks the necessary electron mobility to conduct electricity efficiently.
